beats.prompt: By now you know PHT's answer. Match the pattern to the expected wait it produces via first-step conditioning.

feedback.correct: Right — conditioning on the first flip is the law of total expectation. PHT's E[HH]=6 was its first instance.
hints: Tap a label, then tap its matching value.
PHT's first-step gave E[HH]=6 by conditioning on whether the first flip was H or T.
Match: E[HH] → 6.

beats.prompt: Roll a die. Get {1,2,3}: collect the face and stop. Get {4,5,6}: collect the face and roll again, adding to your total. What is a single play worth?

A little over $3.50.note: Let's test it — re-rolling on 4–6 adds value beyond one roll, so it climbs past $3.50.

About $5.note: Let's test it — warmer, but the re-roll bonus pushes higher. Condition on the first roll and solve.

About $7.note: Good instinct — let's prove it by conditioning on the first roll and solving the self-referential equation.

Unbounded.note: Let's test it — re-rolls get geometrically rarer, so the value converges to a finite number.

beats.prompt: Simpler first. Flip a fair coin: heads → roll a die and keep it; tails → get $0. What is this bet worth? (Give your answer as a fraction, e.g. 3/4.)

feedback.correct: Correct — ½·(7/2) + ½·0 = 7/4. Heads case pays the die's average; tails case pays nothing. Weight each by its probability.
hints: Heads pays the die only half the time. Weight by P(case): ½·3.5 + ½·0 = 7/4.
E[die] = 7/2 = 3.5. Apply total expectation: ½·3.5 + ½·0 = ?

beats.prompt: Back to the dice game. Tap each branch to expand the case tree — then watch the root recombine. One branch loops back.

hero.structuralReadout: E[X] = 7 (½·2 + ½·(5+E[X]) = E[X] solved by isolating E[X]).

feedback.correct: The {4,5,6} branch loops back to the same game — E[X] appears on both sides. The equation ½·2 + ½·(5+E[X]) = E[X] solves to 7.
hints: Tap each branch to see its probability and expected value.
The {4,5,6} branch restarts the game — its value contains E[X] itself.
Once all branches are expanded, the root adds them up. The loop forces you to solve for E[X].

beats.prompt: Those two examples used the same move. Read the law — then tap to confirm you see why averaging is not the same as belief-updating.

interaction.title: Law of Total Expectation
interaction.body: E[X] = Σ E[X|case]·P(case). Split the game into cases → average the value within each case → weight by how likely each case is. If a case restarts the game, E[X] appears on both sides → solve for it. PHT's E[HH]=6 came from the same move (condition on the first flip). Note: conditioning here means averaging over known cases — revising a probability when you see evidence is Bayes, a different machine (coming later).

feedback.correct: Averaging over cases ≠ updating from evidence. Conditioning here: split → average within → weight by P(case). Belief-updating from new evidence is Bayes — a different machine, coming later.
hints: Read the card once more.
Key distinction: here P(case) is known in advance and we average. Bayes revises a probability given evidence.
Continue when you see the difference.

beats.prompt: Set up the dice game equation. The {4,5,6} case: you bank the expected face for that set, then the game restarts. What is that case worth? (Type the full expression, e.g. 5+E[X].)

feedback.correct: Exactly — rolling {4,5,6} banks the expected face (5) and the game replays. That branch is worth 5 + E[X].
hints: Rolling 4–6 banks 5 and restarts the game, so that case is worth 5 + E[X], not just 5.
E[face | {4,5,6}] = (4+5+6)/3 = 5. But the game continues for another full turn.
Write it: case value = 5 + E[X].

beats.prompt: See how E[X] on both sides becomes a clean solve — read each step.

interaction.title: Isolating E[X]
interaction.body: Start: E = ½·2 + ½·(5+E). Expand: E = 1 + 5/2 + E/2. Subtract E/2 from both sides: E − E/2 = 7/2, so ½·E = 7/2. Multiply both sides by 2: E = 7.

feedback.correct: Collect E on one side — it falls out cleanly at 7.
hints: Expand the right side: 1 + 5/2 + E/2.
Subtract E/2 from both sides: E/2 = 7/2.
Multiply by 2: E = 7.

beats.interviewNote: Source: 'play-again' geometric-restart expectation (Green Book, expected-value chapter — the coin/die replay family). Same method as the dice game: condition on the first outcome, let the restart branch carry E[X] on the right, then isolate. The bank-then-replay branch is worth (collected) + E[X], NOT just the collected amount.
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge (fresh): flip a fair coin. Heads → collect $1 and stop. Tails → collect $2 and flip again, adding to your total. Set up and solve the self-referential equation. What is a single play worth?

interaction.scenario: Flip a fair coin. Heads (\(p=\tfrac12\)): collect \$1 and stop. Tails (\(p=\tfrac12\)): collect \$2 and replay, adding to your total. The tails branch is worth \(2 + E[X]\). Set up \(E[X] = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot(2+E[X])\) and solve.

feedback.correct: Condition on the flip and treat \( E[X] \) as the unknown: \[ E[X] = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(2 + E[X]\bigr) \] Expanding: \( \tfrac{1}{2} + 1 + \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = E[X] \), so \( \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2} \), giving \( E[X] = 3 \). The tails branch banks \$2 AND replays, so it carries \(E[X]\) on the right — self-reference is just a linear equation in one unknown.
hints: Tails isn't worth \$2 — it's worth \$2 plus another full play, \(2 + E[X]\). Treat \(E[X]\) as the unknown: \(\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2+E[X]) = E[X]\).
Expand: \( \tfrac{1}{2} + 1 + \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = E[X] \). Collect the \(E[X]\) terms on one side.

beats.interviewNote: E[X] on both sides → isolate and solve (treat E[X] as the unknown: ½·2 + ½·(5+E[X]) = E[X] → E[X] = 7); conditioning ≠ Bayes (here we average over cases, not update from evidence).
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: set up and solve the self-referential dice game equation. What is a single play worth?

interaction.scenario: Roll a die. Get \(\{1,2,3\}\): collect the face and stop. Get \(\{4,5,6\}\): collect the face and roll again, adding to your total. Set up \(E[X] = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot(5+E[X])\) and solve.

feedback.correct: Condition on the first roll and treat \( E[X] \) as the unknown: \[ E[X] = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(5 + E[X]\bigr) \] Expanding: \( 1 + \tfrac{5}{2} + \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = E[X] \), so \( \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = \tfrac{7}{2} \), giving \( E[X] = 7 \). Self-reference is not circular — it is a linear equation in one unknown.
hints: \(E[X]\) on both sides isn't a dead end — treat it as the unknown and solve: \(\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(5+E[X]) = E[X]\).
Expand: \( 1 + \tfrac{5}{2} + \tfrac{E[X]}{2} = E[X] \). Collect \(E[X]\) terms on one side.

beats.prompt: Tap to reveal the lesson's core moves.

feedback.correct: Split the game into cases → average within each → weight by P(case). When a case restarts, E[X] appears on both sides — solve for it. Belief-updating from evidence is Bayes, coming later.
hints: Reveal the cards.
Split → average within → weight by P(case).
If the game loops, solve for E[X].
